answer:
Yes — though not quite in the clean form “people who choose the most interesting job are no happier.” The evidence is more like:

> “Interesting work matters, but people often overestimate how much one salient attribute of a job will determine their day-to-day happiness. Broader factors like autonomy, manager/team quality, workload, social environment, meaning, competence growth, and fit with values also matter a lot.”

A few relevant research strands:

### 1. Interest–job fit predicts satisfaction, but only modestly

In vocational psychology, researchers often study “interest congruence”: how well someone’s interests match their occupation, often using Holland/RIASEC types. Meta-analyses generally find a positive but not huge relationship with job satisfaction — often around **r ≈ .20**, depending on how it’s measured.

That means interest fit explains some variance, but far from most of it. It’s not “irrelevant,” but it’s weaker than many people intuitively expect.

Relevant work: Tranberg, Slane & Ekeberg 1993; Tsabari, Tziner & Meir 2005; broader person–environment fit literature.

### 2. “Maximizing” job choice can backfire

A particularly relevant study is **Iyengar, Wells & Schwartz 2006**, “Doing Better but Feeling Worse.” They studied college graduates looking for jobs. People with a “maximizer” style — trying to find the absolute best possible job — tended to get objectively better outcomes, e.g. higher salaries, but reported **lower satisfaction, more regret, more stress, and more negative affect** than “satisficers.”

This is not exactly “choosing the most interesting job,” but it supports the idea that optimizing heavily on the imagined best option can fail to maximize experienced happiness.

### 3. Affective forecasting: people overpredict the impact of salient features

Daniel Gilbert, Timothy Wilson, Daniel Kahneman and others have shown that people are often bad at predicting how much future circumstances will affect happiness. This includes the “focusing illusion”: when you focus on one variable — location, salary, prestige, interestingness — you tend to overestimate its long-run impact.

For jobs, the thing you vividly imagine may be “the research topic,” but your experienced life may be more driven by things like: meetings, autonomy, interruptions, your manager, politics, stress, sleep, colleagues, and whether you feel effective.

### 4. Work-design research suggests “interesting” is only one part of good work

The job characteristics model and later meta-analyses find that satisfaction and motivation are strongly related to features like:

- autonomy  
- feedback  
- task significance  
- skill variety  
- social support  
- role clarity  
- workload/stress  
- supervisor quality  

“Interesting technical content” overlaps with skill variety and intrinsic motivation, but it’s not the whole picture. A less fascinating domain with high autonomy, growth, and leadership responsibility may be more satisfying than a fascinating domain with lower agency or worse day-to-day conditions.

Relevant work: Hackman & Oldham; Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007; self-determination theory work on autonomy/competence/relatedness.

### 5. Salary also matters, but usually not enormously past a point

Meta-analytic work suggests pay level is only weakly to moderately associated with job satisfaction — often around **r ≈ .15**. So “somewhat higher salary” is nice, but unless it changes your financial security or life options meaningfully, it may not dominate.

Relevant: Judge et al. 2010 on pay and job satisfaction.

---

For your Anthropic vs OpenAI decision, I’d separate “interestingness of the object-level work” from “quality of the life you’ll live in the role.”

Questions I’d ask:

1. **Will leading a small team itself be interesting?**  
   Management can be intellectually rich: strategy, taste, hiring, coaching, coordination, judgment under uncertainty. If that’s something you’ve always wanted, the OpenAI role may be more interesting in practice than it sounds on paper.

2. **Is the OpenAI work “less interesting” or actively boring/misaligned?**  
   Mildly less interesting is different from “I will dread this problem area.”

3. **Can you get leadership opportunities at Anthropic?**  
   If you love the work but want management, it’s worth exploring whether Anthropic can give you a path toward leading a small team.

4. **Who will your manager and teammates be?**  
   This may matter as much as the research agenda.

5. **Which choice creates the better option value?**  
   Leading a team at OpenAI could give you management experience that is hard to get otherwise. Staying at Anthropic could deepen your technical/research edge in work you find unusually meaningful.

6. **Which regret would bother you more?**  
   “I left the most interesting work I’ve ever had” vs. “I passed up the first real chance to lead a team.”

My read of the research: don’t blindly follow “do the most interesting thing possible.” That advice is too one-dimensional. But also don’t dismiss interestingness; it is a real component of motivation and satisfaction. The better target is probably:

> Choose the role with the best expected day-to-day experience, growth, autonomy, people, values alignment, and future option value — not merely the role with the most interesting research topic.

If the OpenAI role is only somewhat less interesting but gives you real leadership, autonomy, excellent people, and stronger career growth, the research would not obviously say “stay for interestingness.” If it is substantially less aligned or you expect chronic boredom, then the higher salary and title may not compensate.

answer:
Yes — though the evidence is more “interesting work matters, but not nearly as much or as cleanly as people imply” than “interesting work doesn’t matter.”

I don’t know of a clean study that randomizes researchers into “most interesting possible job” vs “less interesting but otherwise good job.” But several adjacent literatures are relevant.

## 1. Interest-fit predicts job satisfaction, but only modestly

Vocational psychology has a long tradition around “person–environment fit,” e.g. Holland/RIASEC interest congruence: people are happier and stay longer when their work matches their interests.

Meta-analyses generally find that interest congruence is associated with job satisfaction, but the effect is usually small-to-moderate — often correlations around **r ≈ .20–.30**, depending on measures and samples. See e.g.:

- Assouline & Meir, 1987 — meta-analysis of vocational congruence and well-being/satisfaction.
- Spokane, Meir & Catalano, 2000 — review of person–environment congruence research.
- Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow, 2012/2017 work on vocational interests and outcomes.

A correlation of .20–.30 is real, but it is not destiny. It means “fit with interests” explains some variance in satisfaction, while most variance comes from other things: autonomy, manager quality, workload, coworkers, meaning, stress, compensation, identity, family constraints, etc.

Also, these studies are often observational. Happier people may rate their jobs as more interesting; good jobs may feel more interest-congruent; people may craft jobs over time. So the causal effect of “choose the most interesting job” may be smaller than the raw association.

## 2. Broader job fit matters more than narrow “interestingness”

A broader construct — person–job or person–organization fit — tends to relate more strongly to satisfaction than pure interest fit. But “fit” includes many things:

- values/mission alignment  
- autonomy  
- competence/growth  
- role clarity  
- status/respect  
- compensation/security  
- relationship with manager  
- team culture  
- pace and stress  
- whether the job uses your strengths  

Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson’s 2005 meta-analysis on person–environment fit found that various forms of fit relate substantially to job satisfaction, commitment, and retention. But again, this is not just “I find the technical problem fascinating.”

So if your Anthropic role is much more intellectually interesting but the OpenAI role gives you more autonomy, leadership, growth, influence, and a great team, the overall fit could be closer than “interesting vs not interesting” makes it sound.

## 3. Work design research says autonomy/social context matter a lot

The job characteristics literature — Hackman & Oldham, and later meta-analyses like Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007 — finds that satisfaction and motivation are strongly related to work design features such as:

- autonomy  
- task significance  
- feedback  
- skill variety  
- task identity  
- social support  
- interdependence  
- role clarity  
- manageable workload  

“Interesting work” overlaps with skill variety, challenge, and task significance. But a job can be less intellectually novel while still being highly satisfying if it gives you autonomy, impact, good collaborators, and a sense of competence.

Conversely, a job can be fascinating but miserable if it has chaotic leadership, low agency, political constraints, bad communication, or constant firefighting.

## 4. People often mispredict what will make them happy

Affective forecasting research — e.g. Wilson & Gilbert; Kahneman et al. — suggests people overweight salient, abstract attributes and underweight daily experience.

In career decisions, salient attributes include:

- prestige of institution  
- salary delta  
- title  
- “coolness” of problem  
- identity narrative: “I am the kind of person who does X”  

But day-to-day happiness is often driven by more mundane things:

- Do I like my manager?  
- Do I respect my teammates?  
- Am I constantly interrupted?  
- Do I have enough control over my calendar?  
- Do I get into flow?  
- Am I proud of the work?  
- Do I feel trusted?  
- Am I learning?  
- Is the stress energizing or corrosive?  

There is also a nice related paper by Iyengar, Wells & Schwartz, 2006: **“Doing Better but Feeling Worse.”** They studied job-seeking graduates and found that “maximizers” got objectively better jobs, including higher salaries, but reported worse subjective outcomes than “satisficers.” Not exactly about interestingness, but relevant: optimizing for the “best” job on paper can reduce satisfaction.

## 5. Salary probably matters less at your margin, unless it changes life options

Income does predict well-being, but with diminishing marginal returns/log effects, and the literature is mixed on plateaus. Kahneman & Deaton 2010 found a plateau-ish pattern for emotional well-being; Killingsworth 2021 found continued increases; later reconciliation work suggests the picture depends on baseline happiness.

For someone already highly paid at a frontier AI lab, “a bit higher salary” is probably not a major driver of daily happiness unless it meaningfully changes something concrete: runway, family plans, housing, ability to donate, risk tolerance, etc.

So I would be skeptical of moving mainly for modestly higher salary.

## My read for your specific choice

The evidence does **not** support a simple rule like:

> Always choose the most interesting work.

But it also does **not** support:

> Interestingness is overrated; ignore it.

A better model is:

> You need enough intrinsic interest to stay engaged, but once a role clears that threshold, other factors can dominate.

For you, the key question may be whether “leading a small team” is itself intrinsically interesting. Management/leadership can be deeply satisfying if you enjoy:

- coaching people  
- setting direction  
- making others effective  
- owning outcomes  
- resolving ambiguity  
- building culture  
- translating technical goals into execution  

But if what you actually love is deep technical/research work, the leadership role could mean fewer hours doing the thing you like and more hours in meetings, planning, recruiting, prioritization, performance management, and cross-org alignment.

So I’d try to forecast the actual week, not the abstract job.

Ask yourself or the hiring manager:

- What fraction of my time is technical IC work vs management?  
- Will I set the technical direction or execute someone else’s roadmap?  
- How much autonomy will I have?  
- What does success look like after 6 and 12 months?  
- Who will manage me, and do I trust them?  
- Is the team strong, or am I being hired into a mess?  
- Is the work “less interesting” but still meaningful, or actually boring/misaligned?  
- Could I get a similar leadership path at Anthropic?  
- If I take this and dislike management, can I return to an IC/research role without much damage?  

## Practical heuristic

I’d roughly think in terms of thresholds:

- If the OpenAI role is **below your interest/meaning threshold**, don’t take it for salary/title. You may become disengaged.
- If it is **still interesting enough**, and the leadership/team/autonomy are excellent, it could make you happier than a more intellectually exciting IC role.
- If Anthropic is both much more interesting and also offers good growth/autonomy/people, staying is probably hard to beat unless leadership is a major life/career goal you cannot satisfy there.

The research-backed answer is: yes, people are not automatically much happier just because they picked the most interesting job. Interest fit matters, but modestly; day-to-day autonomy, people, meaning, stress, and fit often matter just as much or more.

answer:
Yes. The research picture is pretty consistent with a nuanced version:

> Doing interesting work matters, but “choose the maximally interesting job” is not strongly supported as a happiness-maximization rule. Interest-fit predicts job satisfaction, but usually only modestly. Broader factors—autonomy, competence/growth, relationships, manager quality, workload, meaning, security, and identity—often matter as much or more.

A few relevant findings:

### 1. Interest/job-fit predicts satisfaction, but not hugely

Vocational psychology has studied “interest congruence”: how well your interests match your job environment, often using Holland/RIASEC models. Meta-analyses generally find positive but modest correlations with job satisfaction—roughly around `r ≈ .20`, depending on measures and corrections.

That means interest-fit is real, but it explains only a small-to-moderate fraction of variance. It is not “if you pick the most interesting possible job, you’ll be much happier.”

Relevant work: Assouline & Meir 1987; Tranberg, Slane & Ekeberg 1993; Hoff et al. 2020 on interest fit and job satisfaction.

### 2. Broader person-job fit is much stronger than “interesting topic” fit

When researchers look at broader person-job fit—your skills, values, needs, work style, autonomy preferences, and goals matching the job—the relationship with job satisfaction is much larger. Meta-analyses often find correlations around `r ≈ .45–.55`.

So “is the research topic fascinating?” is only one part of fit. “Do I get autonomy?”, “am I growing?”, “do I like the people?”, “is the role identity-congruent?”, “do I feel effective?” may matter more.

Relevant: Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005 meta-analysis on person-environment fit.

### 3. Work design factors are very important

The job characteristics literature finds that autonomy, feedback, skill variety, task significance, and task identity are strong predictors of job satisfaction and motivation. In practice, “interesting” may often be a proxy for these things.

A technically less fascinating job that gives you more autonomy, ownership, mentorship responsibility, decision-making power, and social impact could be more satisfying than a more fascinating individual-contributor role with less agency.

Relevant: Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model; Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007 meta-analysis.

### 4. Global happiness may move less than job satisfaction

Even when job satisfaction changes, overall life satisfaction/happiness changes less, because work is only one domain of life and people adapt. Job satisfaction and life satisfaction are correlated—often around `r ≈ .3–.5`—but not identical.

So a more interesting job may make your workdays better without dramatically changing your overall happiness, especially if both options are already high-status, high-pay, high-autonomy jobs.

### 5. Pay probably matters less at your margin, but not zero

Higher salary improves well-being, but the effect is roughly logarithmic/diminishing. At already-high incomes, an incremental raise usually matters less than role quality, stress, autonomy, and growth—unless the money changes something concrete: runway, family plans, location, freedom, donations, etc.

Relevant: Kahneman & Deaton 2010; Killingsworth 2021; Kahneman, Killingsworth & Mellers 2023.

---

## How I’d apply this to your case

The right question may not be:

> “Which job has the more interesting research?”

but rather:

> “Which role will give me the best weekly lived experience and career trajectory?”

For you, the OpenAI offer is not just “more money but less interesting.” It also includes leading a small team, which you say you’ve always wanted. That is a major intrinsic factor: autonomy, impact, mentorship, identity, leadership growth. It may compensate for less technical fascination—or not, depending on what actually energizes you.

I’d try to estimate:

1. **Daily energy**: In a normal week, how many hours would be spent on things you find energizing vs draining?
2. **Autonomy**: Where would you have more control over direction, standards, and decisions?
3. **Growth**: Which role teaches you scarcer skills over the next 2–3 years?
4. **Identity**: Do you want to become a research leader/manager, or mainly a stronger researcher/engineer?
5. **Option value**: Which choice preserves more future paths?
6. **Reversibility**: If you try management and dislike it, can you return to IC research? If you stay, can you get leadership opportunities internally?
7. **Meaning/mission**: Which place’s work will you feel better about having contributed to?

A useful exercise: imagine it is 12 months later.

- You stayed at Anthropic and regret it. Why?
- You joined OpenAI and regret it. Why?
- You stayed and are glad. Why?
- You joined and are glad. Why?

The answers will reveal whether the true variable is “interestingness,” “leadership,” “status,” “mission,” “growth,” or “fear of giving up a rare research environment.”

## Bottom line

There is evidence against the simplistic advice “always choose the most interesting job.” Interest-fit matters, but it is usually a modest predictor of satisfaction. Broader role fit—autonomy, growth, competence, relationships, meaning, workload, and identity—often matters more.

So if the OpenAI role is merely less technically interesting but gives you real leadership, ownership, and growth, it could easily be the better choice. If it is actively dull and mostly management overhead, and your current work feels unusually alive to you, that is a real cost that salary probably will not fully offset.

answer:
Short answer: **yes, there is evidence that “interesting work” is not the whole story and that people often overestimate how much the most salient job attribute will affect happiness.** But the evidence does **not** say interestingness is irrelevant. It says it is one factor among several, and its marginal value may be smaller than people intuitively expect once a job is already intellectually decent.

### What the research roughly says

1. **Interest/job fit predicts satisfaction, but only modestly**
   - Meta-analyses on vocational interest congruence — e.g. Holland-code fit between a person and occupation — generally find **small-to-moderate correlations** with job satisfaction, often around the `.20` range.
   - See work such as Assouline & Meir 1987, Spokane/Meir/Catalano 2000, Nye et al. 2012.
   - Interpretation: people whose work matches their interests are somewhat more satisfied, but the effect is far from overwhelming.

2. **Other job features are often as important or more important**
   Work-design research finds strong associations between satisfaction/engagement and:
   - autonomy,
   - supportive manager/team,
   - competence/mastery,
   - clarity,
   - feedback,
   - workload,
   - social belonging,
   - perceived impact/meaning.

   The Job Characteristics Model literature and meta-analyses such as Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007 generally suggest that **autonomy, feedback, social support, and meaningfulness** can matter a lot. “Interesting topic” is only one route to these.

3. **People are bad affective forecasters**
   Affective forecasting research — Wilson & Gilbert, Kahneman’s “focusing illusion,” etc. — suggests people often overweight the currently salient difference between two options.

   If the salient contrast is:

   > Anthropic = more interesting research  
   > OpenAI = more salary + leadership

   then you may overestimate how much the “interestingness” gap or the “leadership” gap will affect day-to-day happiness. In reality, ordinary Tuesday factors — meetings, autonomy, manager quality, cognitive load, team norms, stress, commute/remote setup, whether you respect the direction — may dominate.

4. **Maximizing can backfire**
   There is a relevant study by Iyengar, Wells & Schwartz 2006 on job seekers: “maximizers” tended to get higher salaries but reported lower satisfaction and more negative affect than “satisficers.” This is not exactly about interesting jobs, but it supports the broader point that optimizing too hard on explicit dimensions can reduce subjective satisfaction.

5. **Salary has diminishing marginal returns, especially at high levels**
   Income does matter, but if both roles already pay very well, a “bit higher” salary may have limited effect on daily happiness unless it changes your financial freedom, risk tolerance, family situation, or long-term optionality.

### The key nuance

Research does **not** support:

> “Pick the most interesting job possible; everything else is secondary.”

But it also does **not** support:

> “Interestingness barely matters.”

A better summary is:

> People are happier when work provides autonomy, competence, relatedness, meaning, manageable stress, and some intrinsic engagement. “Interesting subject matter” helps mostly insofar as it produces those things.

For you, the important distinction is whether the OpenAI role is merely **less intellectually fascinating** or whether it is **less intrinsically motivating overall**.

Leading a small team could itself be interesting if you enjoy:
- mentoring,
- setting direction,
- owning outcomes,
- shaping culture,
- translating messy goals into execution,
- having broader impact.

But if “leading a small team” mostly means meetings, coordination, performance management, and less deep technical work, and that does not energize you, the title may not compensate for the loss of research interest.

### A practical decision rule

I would not ask:

> “Which job is more interesting?”

I would ask:

> “Which job gives me more of the life I want on a normal week?”

Rate both roles on:

| Factor | Anthropic | OpenAI |
|---|---:|---:|
| Intellectual interest |  |  |
| Autonomy |  |  |
| Manager/team quality |  |  |
| Learning rate |  |  |
| Mission/value alignment |  |  |
| Leadership growth |  |  |
| Stress/workload |  |  |
| Ability to do deep work |  |  |
| Long-term optionality |  |  |
| Compensation impact on life |  |  |

Then look especially at the factors with high day-to-day weight: manager, autonomy, workload, team culture, and whether the actual work gives you energy.

### My rough take

If OpenAI is “less interesting” but gives you a genuinely exciting first leadership opportunity with strong autonomy and a team you’d love to lead, it could easily be the better move.

If it is much less interesting, more managerial in a way you are not sure you’ll enjoy, and the salary increase is only marginal relative to your current compensation, then the research would make me cautious about switching just because it looks like an advancement.

A good compromise would be to ask: **can you get a leadership path at Anthropic?** If the main missing thing is team leadership, it may be worth exploring whether you can lead a small pod, manage interns, own a workstream, or transition toward tech lead/manager internally. That would preserve the interesting work while testing the leadership hypothesis.

answer:
Short answer: **yes, the research supports your skepticism—but not the strong version.** “Interesting work” matters, but the evidence does **not** say “maximize intellectual interestingness and you’ll be much happier.” It looks more like: interest fit is one moderately useful predictor among several, and people often over-weight whichever feature is most salient when deciding.

### 1. Interest/job fit predicts satisfaction, but only modestly

The closest literature is on **vocational interest congruence**: do people whose work matches their interests feel more satisfied?

Meta-analyses of Holland/RIASEC-style person–job fit generally find **positive but modest** relationships with job satisfaction—often around **r ≈ .10–.30**, depending on methods and measures. That is real, but it explains only a small-to-moderate fraction of the variance.

Relevant reviews/meta-analyses include:

- Assouline & Meir, 1987, on congruence and well-being/satisfaction.
- Tsabari, Tziner & Meir, 2005, updated meta-analysis on congruence and satisfaction.
- Nye et al., 2012, and Van Iddekinge et al., 2011, on vocational interests and work outcomes.

The takeaway: **working on something that matches your interests helps, but it is far from deterministic.**

### 2. Other job features often matter as much or more

Work-design research finds strong links between satisfaction/motivation and things like:

- **Autonomy**
- **Skill variety**
- **Feedback**
- **Task significance**
- **Good colleagues**
- **Supportive manager**
- **Reasonable workload**
- **Low bureaucracy / low chronic stress**

This comes out of the Job Characteristics Model literature—Hackman & Oldham—and later meta-analyses such as Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007.

So “this topic is fascinating” may be less important than:

> “Do I have autonomy, momentum, trust, competent collaborators, and a sense that my work matters?”

A less intrinsically fascinating role with high autonomy, high trust, strong team, and real leadership growth can feel better than a fascinating role embedded in frustration.

### 3. People often overestimate the impact of one salient factor

Affective forecasting research—Gilbert, Wilson, Kahneman, etc.—suggests that people commonly overestimate how much a single life change will affect their happiness. Kahneman called this the **focusing illusion**:

> “Nothing in life is as important as you think it is while you are thinking about it.”

When deciding between jobs, it is easy to focus on one salient variable: “interestingness,” salary, prestige, leadership, company reputation. But day-to-day happiness is usually driven by the repeated texture of the workweek.

### 4. Job satisfaction has a dispositional component

There is also evidence that job satisfaction is partly stable across jobs and partly influenced by personality/disposition. For example:

- Staw & Ross, 1985, and Staw, Bell & Clausen, 1986, found stability in job attitudes over time.
- Arvey et al., 1989, using twin data, found genetic/dispositional components to job satisfaction.

This does **not** mean jobs don’t matter. They do. But it means switching to the “objectively more interesting” job may not produce as large a happiness change as expected.

### 5. But don’t dismiss interestingness—especially in research

For research roles, intellectual interest can matter more than average because it affects:

- Energy over long ambiguous projects
- Willingness to read deeply and think obsessively
- Creativity
- Taste formation
- Long-term career capital
- Probability of doing exceptional work

So I would not conclude: “interesting work is overrated, ignore it.” I would conclude:

> “Interestingness is important, but it should not be a trump card over autonomy, leadership growth, team quality, stress, mission, and future trajectory.”

### Applying this to your situation

Your choice is not simply:

- Anthropic = interesting
- OpenAI = money

It is more like:

**Current role:** highly interesting research work, likely strong mission/fit, maybe less leadership opportunity.  
**Offer:** somewhat higher salary, explicit small-team leadership, but less interesting technical content.

The key question is whether the OpenAI role is “less interesting” in a tolerable way or in a soul-draining way.

Ask yourself:

1. **Would leading people itself be interesting to me?**  
   If leadership is something you’ve always wanted, the “interestingness” may move from technical content to team-building, strategy, mentoring, and execution.

2. **Is the role genuinely autonomous?**  
   A small-team lead role with real ownership can be great. A “lead” role with little authority and lots of coordination overhead can be frustrating.

3. **Can you get the leadership path at Anthropic?**  
   Before deciding, it may be worth having a candid conversation internally: “I’m increasingly interested in leading a small technical team. Is there a path to that here?”

4. **How reversible is the move?**  
   If trying management for 12–18 months is reversible, the option value is high. If leaving your current research area would be hard to undo, that matters.

5. **Which regret would be worse?**  
   In two years, would you more regret:
   - leaving unusually interesting work, or
   - not trying leadership when you had a concrete chance?

### My read from the research

The literature supports this position:

> You probably should not choose purely based on “most interesting topic.” People are not reliably much happier just because they maximized interest fit. But if the role is substantially less interesting and that would reduce your energy, taste, and long-term excellence, that is a serious cost.

So I would treat “interestingness” as a major variable, not the variable.

A good practical heuristic:

- If the OpenAI role has **real leadership, high autonomy, excellent people, sane workload, and decent technical depth**, it may be worth serious consideration even if the topic is less fascinating.
- If it is **less interesting plus more politics, less autonomy, unclear mandate, or management-in-name-only**, I would be cautious.
- If your current work gives you rare intellectual aliveness and you can plausibly get leadership experience where you are, staying may be the better bet.

answer:
Short answer: **yes, there is evidence against “just pick the most interesting job possible” as a reliable happiness-maximizer** — but not evidence that interesting work is unimportant. The research picture is more like:

> Interest/fit matters, often a lot for job satisfaction.  
> But it is only one contributor, and people can overweight it relative to autonomy, social environment, stress, management quality, identity, growth, and day-to-day schedule.

For your case, “less interesting but leading a small team, higher pay, and a role you’ve wanted” is very plausibly not dominated by “more interesting IC research.”

## What the research roughly says

### 1. “Interest fit” predicts job satisfaction, but usually only moderately

Vocational psychology has studied “person–job fit” and “interest congruence”: do your interests match your work? Meta-analyses generally find a positive but not overwhelming association with satisfaction.

Examples:

- **Assouline & Meir 1987**, **Tranberg et al. 1993**, and later work on Holland interest congruence find correlations with satisfaction often around the **.2–.3** range. That is real, but not “this determines happiness.”
- Broader **person–job fit** measures, which include whether the job meets your needs and uses your abilities, predict job satisfaction more strongly. **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005** found person–job fit was substantially related to job satisfaction. But that is broader than “is the technical problem maximally interesting?”

So: doing work you find interesting helps. But “most interesting possible” is not clearly the optimum.

### 2. Work design factors can matter as much or more than topical interest

The job characteristics literature finds that things like:

- autonomy,
- feedback,
- task significance,
- skill variety,
- task identity,
- social support,
- workload,
- role clarity,

are consistently linked to job satisfaction and motivation.

A major meta-analysis, **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007**, found that motivational and social characteristics of work are robust predictors of satisfaction and well-being.

This is relevant because “leading a small team” may increase some of these: autonomy, influence, task significance, mentoring, ownership, status, and feedback loops. It may also worsen others: meetings, interruptions, people problems, less deep work.

So the key question is not only “is the research direction interesting?” but “what is my actual lived workday like?”

### 3. Pay matters, but less than people often think once you are already highly paid

A meta-analysis by **Judge et al. 2010** found that pay level has only a small-to-moderate relationship with job satisfaction. Money matters more when it changes security, freedom, family options, location, or retirement trajectory. If the salary difference is “nice but not life-changing,” it probably should not dominate the decision.

For someone already in a high-compensation AI research role, the marginal salary increase may matter less than:

- team quality,
- manager quality,
- freedom,
- mission fit,
- burnout risk,
- learning,
- long-term trajectory.

### 4. People are not great at predicting what will make them happy

Affective forecasting research — e.g. **Wilson & Gilbert** and the “focusing illusion” work by **Kahneman et al.** — suggests people often overweight the most salient difference between options.

When comparing jobs, the salient differences may be:

- “This research is more interesting.”
- “This title is better.”
- “This salary is higher.”
- “This company is more prestigious.”
- “I get to lead.”

But happiness often comes from mundane repeated factors:

- Do I like my manager?
- Do I respect the team?
- Do I have control over my calendar?
- Am I constantly interrupted?
- Do I feel psychologically safe?
- Do I get enough deep work?
- Are the meetings energizing or draining?
- Do I feel proud of the mission?
- Am I learning?
- Do I sleep well?

A job can win on the explicit comparison dimension and lose on the experienced-life dimension.

### 5. “Meaning” and “calling” can help, but can also be costly

Research on meaningful work and calling generally finds positive associations with job satisfaction and engagement. But there is also “dark side of calling” literature: people may tolerate worse conditions, overwork, or exploitation because the work feels important.

In frontier AI, “interesting and important” can easily become “I should endure anything because this matters.” So it is worth separating:

- genuine intellectual aliveness,
- moral importance,
- identity/status,
- obligation,
- fear of missing out.

Those are different.

## My read for your decision

I would not interpret the evidence as saying:

> “Take the most interesting research job; interest is what matters.”

Nor would I interpret it as:

> “Interest is overrated; take the leadership role.”

The better model is:

> You need a sufficient level of intrinsic interest and meaning. Past that threshold, role shape, autonomy, people, growth, and sustainability may dominate.

For your specific choice, I’d ask:

### If you join OpenAI, what exactly becomes less interesting?

There are two very different cases.

**Case A: Less interesting topic, but still meaningful and technically adjacent.**  
Then the leadership opportunity could be genuinely worth it. You may get satisfaction from growing people, setting direction, owning outcomes, and operating at a higher leverage point.

**Case B: Less interesting in a way that makes you feel detached, cynical, or like you are no longer doing the work you came to AI for.**  
Then the leadership title may not compensate. Managing a team on work you do not care about can become draining quickly.

### Do you want to lead, or do you want to have led?

This is important.

Leading a small team often means:

- less uninterrupted research time,
- more meetings,
- more ambiguity,
- responsibility for others’ motivation and performance,
- hiring, planning, review cycles,
- conflict resolution,
- spending time on communication rather than technical depth.

Some people love that. Some people love the idea of it but miss the craft. If you “always wanted to lead,” this offer may be a valuable test — but you should be honest about whether you want the daily reality.

### Can you get leadership where you are?

Before choosing, I would consider explicitly asking Anthropic something like:

> “I’m excited about my current research direction, but I’m increasingly motivated to grow into leading a small team. Is there a path to that here over the next 6–12 months?”

If your current role is much more interesting and you can also get a leadership path, that may dominate. If the answer is vague or no, the OpenAI offer becomes more attractive.

### Do a “Tuesday test”

Imagine an ordinary Tuesday six months from now.

At Anthropic:

- What are you doing from 10am–6pm?
- How much deep work?
- How much excitement?
- How much frustration?
- Are you growing, or just doing more of the same?

At OpenAI:

- How many hours of meetings?
- How much technical involvement?
- Are you shaping direction?
- Do you like the team?
- Are you energized by helping others succeed?
- Do you feel ownership, or are you middle-managing a less compelling agenda?

The ordinary Tuesday is probably more predictive than the abstract title or research topic.

## A practical decision rule

I’d suggest something like this:

1. **Do not choose primarily for the salary** unless the difference materially changes your life.
2. **Do not choose purely for maximal interestingness** unless the OpenAI work falls below your “I can care about this” threshold.
3. **Put heavy weight on manager/team/culture**, because the evidence and common experience both suggest these matter enormously.
4. **Treat leadership as a real preference**, not a superficial perk. If you have wanted it for a long time, that is meaningful.
5. **Try to negotiate toward the missing dimension**:
   - Ask OpenAI for a more technically interesting charter.
   - Ask Anthropic for a concrete leadership path.

If OpenAI can make the scope at least “interesting enough,” and the team/manager are strong, taking the leadership role could be very reasonable. If the work feels genuinely dull or misaligned, I would be cautious: management amplifies your relationship to the work rather than replacing it.

answer:
Short answer: **yes—there’s quite a bit of evidence against “maximize interestingness at all costs.”** But the evidence does *not* say interesting work is unimportant. It says something closer to:

> Intrinsic interest/person–job fit matters, but job happiness is also strongly driven by autonomy, competence/growth, social environment, manager quality, values fit, workload, fairness, and life circumstances. The marginal gain from “the most interesting possible topic” may be smaller than people imagine, especially once a job is already “interesting enough.”

## What the research roughly says

### 1. Interest fit matters, but usually modestly
Vocational psychology studies on **person–environment fit** and **interest congruence**—e.g. Holland-style “does this work match my interests?”—generally find positive but not gigantic relationships with satisfaction.

Meta-analyses such as Assouline & Meir 1987, Spokane et al. 2000, and later work on vocational interests typically find correlations in the **small-to-moderate** range. Depending on measurement, interest congruence often predicts job satisfaction around **r ≈ .10–.30**. That is real, but far from “this dominates everything.”

Broader **person–job fit** has stronger associations—Kristof-Brown et al. 2005 find quite large relationships with job satisfaction—but that includes not only interest, also abilities, needs, values, work conditions, rewards, and role fit.

So: “I find the subject fascinating” helps, but “this job fits how I want to work and live” matters more.

### 2. Work design features are very strong predictors
The job-design literature—Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model, and meta-analyses like Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007—finds that satisfaction and motivation are strongly related to things like:

- **Autonomy**
- **Feedback**
- **Skill variety**
- **Task significance / impact**
- **Task identity**
- **Social support**
- **Reasonable workload**
- **Good management**
- **Clarity and fairness**

This is important for your case because “leading a small team” may give you more autonomy, feedback loops, impact, mentoring, and skill growth—even if the technical content is less intrinsically exciting.

In other words, the OpenAI role may be “less interesting” along one axis but more satisfying along others.

### 3. Salary matters, but not as much as people expect—especially at high incomes
Pay is correlated with satisfaction, but the relationship between **absolute pay level** and job satisfaction is usually modest. Judge et al. 2010, for example, found pay level to have a relatively small association with job satisfaction.

Income does predict life satisfaction, and recent work by Killingsworth and others suggests the relationship does not fully plateau as early as once thought. But at already-high tech/research salaries, the difference between “very high” and “somewhat higher” may matter less than:

- whether you enjoy your day-to-day work,
- whether the role builds career capital,
- whether you feel respected/autonomous,
- whether you like your manager/team,
- whether the job aligns with your values.

A higher salary is still real value—optionality, savings, family, freedom—but it probably should not be the only deciding factor.

### 4. People adapt; job changes have “honeymoon/hangover” dynamics
There is research on job changes showing a “honeymoon–hangover” effect: satisfaction often rises after a move and then partially declines as novelty wears off, e.g. Boswell, Boudreau & Tichy 2005.

This cuts both ways:

- The excitement of higher pay/title/team leadership may fade.
- The excitement of “fascinating research” can also fade if other job features are bad or stagnant.

So the key is not “which option feels exciting now?” but “which has better enduring daily conditions?”

## How I’d apply this to your choice

I would not frame it as:

> Interesting work vs. money.

I’d frame it as:

> Deep technical fascination vs. leadership growth, autonomy, impact, compensation, and future trajectory.

The big question is whether the OpenAI job is **less interesting but still interesting enough**, or **so much less interesting that you’ll feel your talent is being spent on the wrong thing**.

Those are very different.

### If the OpenAI role is “interesting enough”
Then taking it could be very reasonable. Leadership is not just a perk; it is a different source of intrinsic motivation. If you have always wanted to lead a small team, that can satisfy needs for autonomy, competence, impact, and mentorship. Those are strongly connected to work satisfaction.

Also, early management experience can be career-defining. If you want to become a research/engineering lead, the first serious opportunity to lead a team may be worth a lot.

### If the role is genuinely boring or misaligned
Then be careful. For someone in research engineering, intrinsic curiosity is not merely a happiness variable—it affects energy, creativity, taste, and persistence. If the new work is “less interesting” in the sense of “I would not be proud or energized by this,” the salary and title may not compensate for long.

## A practical decision rule

I’d use a threshold model rather than a maximization model:

1. **Is the work above my minimum bar for intrinsic interest?**  
   If no, probably don’t take it.

2. **Does the role give me a scarce growth opportunity?**  
   Leading a small team sounds like yes.

3. **Would I still want the job if the salary were the same?**  
   If yes, strong signal. If no, ask whether salary alone is doing too much work.

4. **Can I get the same leadership path at Anthropic?**  
   Before deciding, it may be worth exploring internally: “I’m interested in leading a small team; what path exists here?”

5. **Which regret is more painful?**  
   - Staying: “I kept doing fascinating work but delayed becoming a lead.”
   - Leaving: “I got title/money but left the most interesting work of my life.”

6. **How reversible is the decision?**  
   If you leave and dislike it, could you return to similar frontier research? If yes, experimenting is less costly. If no, staying has more option value.

## My read from the research

The research does **not** support the folk advice “just pick the most interesting possible thing.” It supports something more balanced:

> Choose work that is sufficiently interesting, gives autonomy and growth, has good people/management, and fits the kind of person you want to become.

So if Anthropic is dramatically more interesting *and* you can plausibly grow into leadership there, staying may be better. But if the OpenAI role is still meaningfully interesting and gives you the leadership experience you’ve wanted, the evidence would not say you’re making a happiness mistake by choosing it.

answer:
Short answer: **yes, in a qualified way**. The research does *not* say “interesting work doesn’t matter.” It says something more like: **interest fit helps, but it is usually not the dominant determinant of happiness; autonomy, competence growth, relationships, workload, meaning, manager quality, and day-to-day work design often matter as much or more.** So “choose the most interesting possible job” is probably too simplistic.

### What the research roughly says

1. **Vocational-interest fit predicts job satisfaction, but modestly.**  
   Meta-analyses on Holland-style “person–environment fit” / vocational-interest congruence generally find positive but not huge relationships with job satisfaction — often around **r ≈ .10–.25**, depending on method. That is real, but it explains only a small-to-moderate fraction of variance.  
   Relevant work: Assouline & Meir 1987; Tranberg, Slane & Ekeberg 1993; Spokane 1985; Nye/Su/Rounds/Drasgow work on vocational interests.

2. **Global person–job fit is more strongly related to satisfaction, but it includes many things besides “interesting topic.”**  
   Meta-analyses of perceived person–job fit often find larger correlations with job satisfaction — sometimes around **r ≈ .4–.6** — but this is partly because “this job fits me” includes autonomy, values, skills, environment, identity, etc., and is often measured cross-sectionally.  
   Relevant: Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005.

3. **Work design variables are very important.**  
   The Job Characteristics Model literature finds that **autonomy, skill variety, task significance, feedback, social support, and manageable demands** are robustly related to job satisfaction, motivation, and engagement. These may matter more for daily happiness than whether the research topic is maximally fascinating.  
   Relevant: Hackman & Oldham; Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007.

4. **Self-determination theory points to autonomy, competence, and relatedness.**  
   People tend to do better when work satisfies:
   - **Autonomy**: I have real agency.
   - **Competence**: I am growing/mastering something.
   - **Relatedness**: I like/respect the people around me.  
   “Interesting research” often gives competence and autonomy, but **leading a team** might also give those, plus relatedness/status/impact.  
   Relevant: Deci & Ryan; Van den Broeck et al. 2016.

5. **People often overpredict the long-term happiness impact of a change.**  
   Affective forecasting research suggests people overestimate how much a concrete change — new job, new city, higher status, etc. — will affect long-run happiness. There is also evidence of “honeymoon-hangover” effects after job changes: satisfaction may rise initially and then partially normalize.  
   Relevant: Wilson & Gilbert; Boswell et al. 2005.

### The practical takeaway

I would not interpret the evidence as “interestingness is overrated and you should ignore it.” Rather:

> **Going from boring work to genuinely interesting work matters a lot.  
> Going from very interesting work to slightly more interesting work may matter less than autonomy, team quality, leadership growth, stress, and mission fit.**

So in your case, the key question may not be “Which job has the more interesting technical problem?” but:

**Which job gives you the better day-to-day life and growth trajectory?**

For the OpenAI role, ask yourself:

- Would leading a small team be intrinsically interesting to me, or do I mostly want the *idea/status* of leading?
- Would I still get enough technical depth/novelty, or would I become mostly a coordinator?
- How much autonomy would I have over research direction?
- Is the manager/org context good?
- Is the team excellent?
- Are the success criteria sane?
- Is the “less interesting” work merely less intellectually sexy, or actually draining?
- Does the role build a capability I strongly want — research leadership — that your current role does not?

For the Anthropic role, ask:

- Is the current work unusually rare and energizing?
- Could you negotiate more leadership internally?
- Are you learning at the rate you want?
- Would you regret leaving this research area specifically?
- Are you staying because it is truly good, or because switching feels risky?

A useful heuristic: **satisfice on interest, then optimize for autonomy, people, growth, and values.** If the OpenAI work is still “interesting enough” and the leadership path is real, the research does not clearly say you should stay just because Anthropic’s work is more fascinating. But if the OpenAI role would make your typical Tuesday much less alive — more politics, less deep work, less mission alignment — then the salary/title bump may not compensate.

If possible, I’d also explore a third option: **tell Anthropic you want to lead a small team**. If they can give you the leadership growth while preserving the more interesting work, that may dominate both options.

answer:
Short answer: **yes, the evidence is consistent with “interesting work matters, but not nearly enough to maximize it at all costs.”** Research generally finds that fit with one’s interests predicts job satisfaction and persistence, but the effect is usually **modest** compared with the whole bundle of work conditions: autonomy, manager/team quality, workload, social support, meaning, competence growth, values fit, and day-to-day stress.

## What the research roughly says

### 1. Interest/job fit predicts satisfaction, but not overwhelmingly
Vocational-psychology meta-analyses on “interest congruence” — e.g. whether your job matches your Holland/RIASEC interests — typically find correlations with job satisfaction around **r ≈ .15–.25**. That is real, but it explains only a few percent of variance.

Examples often cited include work by Assouline & Meir, Tranberg et al., Spokane et al., and later reviews/meta-analyses on person–environment or interest fit. The takeaway: **people whose work matches their interests are somewhat more satisfied, more likely to persist, and sometimes perform better, but interest match is far from destiny.**

So if “most interesting possible” means “maximally aligned with my intellectual curiosity,” research does **not** imply that this will dominate everything else.

### 2. Work design factors often matter as much or more
Meta-analyses of job design, such as Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007, find strong associations between job satisfaction and things like:

- **Autonomy / control**
- **Skill variety**
- **Task significance / impact**
- **Feedback**
- **Social support**
- **Good management**
- **Manageable demands**

A less intellectually fascinating job with much more autonomy, leadership, impact, and better social context can plausibly be happier than a maximally interesting IC role with less agency or growth.

### 3. Salary matters, but marginal salary increases usually matter less than people expect
Pay is correlated with job satisfaction, but weakly-to-moderately, and there are diminishing returns, especially once your needs and financial goals are covered. A “bit higher salary” is probably not the main variable unless it meaningfully changes your security, optionality, or life plans.

### 4. People are bad at forecasting what will make them happy
Affective forecasting research — Gilbert, Wilson, Kahneman/Schkade-style “focusing illusion” results — suggests people often overweight salient features of a choice. In job decisions, this might mean overweighting:

- “This research topic is fascinating”
- “The title is team lead”
- “The salary is higher”
- “OpenAI/Anthropic brand”

…and underweighting the boring-but-important daily realities: meetings, manager, interruptions, politics, hiring burden, evaluation criteria, hours, stress, how much uninterrupted thinking time you get, etc.

## Applying this to your case

I would not frame the decision as:

> “Should I choose the most interesting work or not?”

I’d frame it as:

> “Which role gives me the better expected next 2–3 years across curiosity, autonomy, growth, impact, people, stress, values, and future options?”

The OpenAI offer has at least three distinct components:

1. **Higher salary** — probably nice but maybe not decisive.
2. **Leading a small team** — potentially very important if management/leadership is a real aspiration.
3. **Less interesting work** — a real cost, but maybe not fatal if the work is still above some “interesting enough” threshold.

The key question is whether “less interesting” means:

- **Still intellectually alive, but not your favorite topic** — probably fine if leadership/autonomy are strong.
- **Genuinely dull/draining/low-conviction** — dangerous; title and salary may not compensate.
- **Misaligned with your values or taste in research** — also dangerous.
- **Less interesting as research, but interesting as organizational/strategic leadership** — could be a great growth move.

## Questions I’d ask before deciding

For the OpenAI role:

- What would my actual weekly calendar look like?
- What fraction is technical direction vs people management vs coordination?
- Would I still do hands-on research/engineering?
- How much autonomy would I have over the team’s agenda?
- Who is my manager, and do I trust their taste?
- Is the team strong enough that leading them would be energizing?
- What are the success metrics?
- If I dislike management after 6–12 months, can I move back to IC?
- Is the project “less interesting” but important, or merely less interesting and lower-conviction?
- Would I take this role if the salary were identical?

For Anthropic:

- Can I get a path to leading a small team or subproject internally?
- Is my current work unusually interesting in a way I’d regret giving up?
- Am I staying because the work is great, or because switching is scary?
- Will my current role still be as interesting in a year?
- Can I negotiate more leadership/responsibility where I am?

## A practical decision rule

I’d roughly use this:

- **Stay at Anthropic** if the current work feels rare, energizing, and high-conviction, and the OpenAI work would be merely “fine” plus title/salary.
- **Take OpenAI** if leading a team is a deep career goal, the role has real autonomy and good people, and the work is at least interesting enough.
- **Do not move primarily for a small salary bump.**
- **Do not stay solely because “interesting work is what one is supposed to optimize.”** The literature does not support maximizing interestingness while ignoring leadership, agency, and environment.

The most research-consistent view is: **interestingness matters a lot as a threshold variable — avoid work you find deadening — but beyond “pretty interesting,” other factors can dominate happiness and career growth.**

answer:
Short answer: **yes—there is evidence against the “just choose the most interesting work possible” theory of happiness.** But the evidence is not “interest doesn’t matter.” It is more like:

> Intellectual interest / passion / vocational fit matters, but usually only moderately. Day-to-day autonomy, competence growth, social environment, manager quality, workload, meaning, and future option value often matter as much or more.

For your specific situation, I would not treat “less interesting” as automatically disqualifying—especially if leading a small team is genuinely something you want to learn. But I also would not move for “slightly more salary + management title” if the day-to-day work would feel dull and politically constrained.

## What the research says

### 1. Interest-job fit predicts satisfaction, but not overwhelmingly

There is a long literature on “person–environment fit,” especially Holland’s vocational interest theory. Meta-analyses generally find that people are more satisfied when their interests fit their job, but the effect is usually **modest** rather than huge.

Examples:

- Assouline & Meir, 1987; Spokane, 1985; Tranberg et al., 1993; later reviews on interest congruence generally find correlations around **r ≈ .20–.30** between interest fit and satisfaction/well-being.
- Hoff et al., 2020, in a modern review/meta-analysis, similarly conclude that interest fit matters, but it is far from deterministic.

A correlation of .20–.30 is real, but it means “interest fit explains some variance,” not “pick the most interesting job and you will be much happier.”

By contrast, broader **person-job fit**—which includes not just interest but whether the job meets your needs, uses your abilities, matches your values, gives rewards you want, etc.—has larger associations with job satisfaction. Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005 found much stronger relationships between person-job fit and job satisfaction, roughly in the **moderate-to-large** range. But that is broader than “is the technical topic fascinating?”

### 2. Work design often matters more than abstract topic interest

The job characteristics literature finds robust links between satisfaction/motivation and things like:

- autonomy,
- task significance,
- skill variety,
- feedback,
- social support,
- reasonable workload,
- clear goals.

Classic source: Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model. A major meta-analysis by Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007 found that motivational and social features of work are strongly related to satisfaction and motivation.

This matters for your case because “interesting research topic” is only one part of your experienced work. A technically less interesting role with much more autonomy, real ownership, great colleagues, and leadership growth could be more satisfying than a fascinating IC role with less agency. Conversely, a “team lead” role with little authority, many meetings, weak mandate, and dull product pressure could be worse despite salary/title.

### 3. People are bad at forecasting what will make them happy

Affective forecasting research—e.g. Wilson & Gilbert—shows that people often overestimate how much salient differences will affect their future happiness. In job decisions, the salient dimensions are usually:

- salary,
- company prestige,
- title,
- apparent interestingness of the domain.

But the experienced dimensions are often:

- calendar shape,
- who your manager is,
- whether your work is respected,
- whether you are constantly context-switching,
- whether you feel competent,
- whether you are learning,
- how much conflict/politics there is,
- whether you like the people you spend 30–50 hours/week with.

So it is plausible that you are over-weighting “interestingness,” but it is also plausible that you are under-weighting it if intellectual excitement is a core part of your identity.

### 4. “Passion” is partly discovered and partly developed

There is also research suggesting that interest is not fixed. O’Keefe, Dweck & Walton 2018, for example, argue that a “fixed theory of passion”—the idea that you should simply find the thing you already love—can be misleading. Interests can deepen through mastery, responsibility, and contribution.

This is relevant because leading a team may become interesting in a different way. The intellectual object might be less exciting, but the system you are optimizing becomes larger: people, strategy, execution, taste, judgment, culture, mentorship. If that appeals to you, the OpenAI role may not be “less interesting” so much as “interesting along a different axis.”

### 5. Salary helps, but marginal salary differences are often not decisive

Income is associated with life satisfaction, and recent work such as Killingsworth 2021 finds that experienced well-being continues to rise with income more than older “plateau” interpretations suggested. But the relationship is roughly logarithmic: a “bit higher” salary may matter if it materially changes your savings, family security, or freedom, but it is unlikely by itself to compensate for a substantially worse day-to-day role.

So I would treat salary as a tiebreaker unless the difference is large enough to change your life strategy.

## How I would frame your decision

You are not choosing between:

> interesting work vs happiness.

You are choosing among several goods:

1. **Deep technical interest**  
   Are you energized by the actual research problems at Anthropic? Would losing that hurt a lot?

2. **Leadership development**  
   Is leading a small team a central career goal or just an appealing status marker? Do you want the actual work of management: hiring, performance feedback, coordination, conflict resolution, prioritization, being interrupted, taking responsibility for others’ output?

3. **Autonomy and scope**  
   At OpenAI, would you have real decision rights? Or would you be a “lead” mostly in name?

4. **People and manager quality**  
   Who will you report to? Are they excellent? Will they protect your team? Will you learn from them?

5. **Mission/value alignment**  
   Given the Anthropic/OpenAI context, do you feel more aligned with one lab’s strategy, culture, or safety posture? This may matter more than abstract “interestingness.”

6. **Career option value**  
   Two years from now, which path gives you better future choices? A stronger research track record? Management experience? Broader network? Better judgment?

7. **Reversibility**  
   If you try leading and dislike it, can you return to IC research? If you stay, can you get a leadership opportunity later?

## Practical test

I would ask both yourself and OpenAI very concrete questions.

For OpenAI:

- What percentage of my time would be hands-on technical work vs people management?
- Do I have authority over roadmap, hiring, performance reviews, and technical direction?
- What are the team’s actual goals for the next 6–12 months?
- Why is this team strategically important?
- Who is my manager, and how do they support new leads?
- What would make me successful or unsuccessful after one year?
- Can I talk to current or former people in similar roles?
- Is there a path to move toward more interesting research areas later?

For yourself:

- In my happiest weeks at Anthropic, what specifically made them good?
- In my worst weeks, what specifically made them bad?
- Do I want to lead because I enjoy growing people and setting direction, or because I feel I “should”?
- If the OpenAI role had the same salary as Anthropic, would I still be tempted?
- If the OpenAI role did not come with a lead title, would I still be tempted?
- If Anthropic offered me a credible path to lead a small team within 6–12 months, would I stay?

That last question is especially important. If the main attraction is leadership, you might first see whether you can get more leadership scope where the work is already fascinating.

## My tentative read

Based only on what you wrote:

- If the OpenAI work is **only moderately less interesting**, and the team-lead role is real, supported, and strategically meaningful, then taking it could be very reasonable. Leadership may become a new source of intrinsic motivation.
- If the OpenAI work is **much less interesting**, the salary bump is only modest, and the leadership role is ambiguous, I would be cautious. Research suggests interest is not everything, but it is still a real component of satisfaction—especially for people whose identity is strongly tied to intellectual work.
- If Anthropic can offer a path toward leadership while preserving the interesting work, that may dominate both options.

So the evidence supports a nuanced conclusion:

> Do not maximize “interestingness” blindly. But do not dismiss it either. Optimize for the whole experienced job: autonomy, growth, people, mission, workload, leadership scope, and the kind of person you want to become.

answer:
Short answer: **yes, there’s evidence suggesting “the most interesting job” is not reliably the thing that maximizes happiness**—but the evidence is indirect, and it does *not* imply interesting work is unimportant.

A better summary of the literature is:

> Interesting work matters, but it is only one component of job well-being. Autonomy, manager/team quality, workload, values fit, growth, status/fairness, and day-to-day affect often matter as much or more. People also tend to overforecast how much one salient feature of a job will affect their happiness.

## What the research says

### 1. “Interest fit” predicts satisfaction, but modestly

Vocational psychology has studied “interest congruence”: how well your job matches your interests, often using Holland/RIASEC-type measures. Meta-analyses generally find that interest fit is positively related to job satisfaction, but the relationship is usually **small to moderate**, often around **r ≈ .15–.25** depending on measurement.

That means it matters, but it does not dominate everything else. It explains only a modest fraction of variance in satisfaction.

By contrast, broader **person–job fit**—including skills, needs, values, rewards, and working conditions—has much stronger associations with satisfaction. Kristof-Brown et al. 2005, for example, found fairly large correlations between overall fit and job satisfaction. But that’s broader than “I find the subject matter intellectually fascinating.”

### 2. Job design factors are very robust predictors

The work-design literature, e.g. Hackman & Oldham’s job characteristics model and later meta-analyses like Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007, finds that people tend to be more satisfied when they have:

- autonomy,
- skill variety,
- task significance,
- feedback,
- social support,
- reasonable workload,
- good manager/team environment.

So a “less intellectually fascinating” job with high autonomy, strong people, real ownership, and meaningful responsibility can be very satisfying. Conversely, a fascinating job with low autonomy, chronic pressure, or bad management can be miserable.

For your specific case, “leading a small team” may score highly on autonomy, ownership, impact, relatedness, and growth—even if the object-level research is less exciting.

### 3. People are bad at forecasting how job features will affect happiness

Affective forecasting research—Wilson & Gilbert, Kahneman’s “focusing illusion,” etc.—suggests that people often overweight the feature they are currently thinking about and underweight mundane repeated experiences.

Classic example: students overpredicted how much dorm assignment/location would affect happiness; actual well-being depended more on social life and day-to-day interactions.

Applied to jobs: you might overfocus on “this research agenda is more interesting” or “this salary is higher” and underweight things like:

- Who is my manager?
- Do I respect my peers?
- How much agency do I have?
- How many meetings will I have?
- Will I get into flow?
- Will I be proud of what I’m building?
- Will I feel trusted?
- Will I be constantly stressed?

These repeated daily factors often matter more than the headline reason you chose the job.

### 4. More salary probably has limited direct happiness effect at your level

Income is associated with well-being, but at high incomes the marginal effect is usually not huge, especially for day-to-day emotional experience. It can still matter through security, freedom, status, bargaining power, family goals, or ability to take risks later. But if both offers are already well above comfort/security thresholds, salary probably should not dominate unless the difference changes your life materially.

### 5. “Interesting” may be more about meaning than happiness

A very interesting research role may not make you dramatically happier moment-to-moment, but it can matter for:

- identity,
- meaning,
- long-term pride,
- intellectual growth,
- regret avoidance,
- career capital,
- sense of being at the frontier.

So the right question may not be “which job makes me happiest?” but:

> Which role gives me the life/career trajectory I actually want?

## How I’d apply this to your decision

I would not reduce it to:

> Anthropic = interesting, OpenAI = salary + management.

Instead ask what your **actual days** would look like.

For each option, estimate:

1. **Daily energy**: What fraction of the week would I look forward to?
2. **Autonomy**: Can I shape the agenda, or am I executing someone else’s plan?
3. **Learning curve**: Am I growing in a direction I care about?
4. **People**: Do I trust and enjoy the manager, peers, and reports?
5. **Impact/values fit**: Will I feel good about the work?
6. **Stress/load**: What are the real expectations?
7. **Reversibility**: If I try management and dislike it, can I return to IC research?
8. **Regret**: In two years, would I more regret giving up fascinating research, or missing the chance to lead?

A particularly useful test:

> Imagine a totally normal Tuesday six months into each job. Calendar, Slack, meetings, coding/research time, decisions, interpersonal issues. Which Tuesday do you want?

Also: if leading a team is “something you always wanted to do,” be careful not to classify the OpenAI job as simply “less interesting.” It may be less interesting along the *research-content* axis, but more interesting along the *agency/leadership/org-building* axis.

## Bottom line

The evidence does **not** support “always pick the most intellectually interesting job.” Interest fit helps, but it is not overwhelmingly predictive of happiness. People often adapt, and job satisfaction depends heavily on autonomy, relationships, workload, values, and daily work design.

But the evidence also does **not** support ignoring interestingness—especially for someone like you, whose work identity may be deeply tied to research taste and intellectual frontier work.

My practical take: choose the role whose **day-to-day life and long-term trajectory** you want, not the one with the best single attribute. And before deciding, get very concrete about what the OpenAI leadership role actually entails versus whether Anthropic can offer a path toward team leadership without sacrificing the research you find most compelling.

answer:
Yes. The evidence broadly supports a nuanced version of your suspicion:

> **Doing work you find interesting matters, but “maximizing interestingness” is not reliably the thing that maximizes happiness — especially once you are already in a pretty good, high-autonomy, high-skill job.**

A few relevant research findings:

### 1. Interest/job fit predicts satisfaction, but only modestly

Vocational psychology has studied “person–job fit” or “interest congruence” for decades — e.g. whether someone with investigative/scientific interests ends up in an investigative/scientific job.

Meta-analyses generally find that interest fit is positively related to job satisfaction, but the correlations are not enormous — often around **r ≈ .10–.30**, depending on measurement and sample. Classic examples include work by **Assouline & Meir 1987**, **Spokane 1985**, and later meta-analytic work in the Holland/RIASEC tradition.

Interpretation: yes, being in a job that fits your interests helps. But it is not like “choose the most fascinating possible job and you will be dramatically happier.” It explains a meaningful but limited fraction of variance.

This is especially important in your case because you are comparing two elite AI research roles, not “AI research vs accounting.” The marginal difference in interestingness may feel large when you focus on it, but the actual happiness difference may be smaller than expected.

### 2. Other job features often matter as much or more

Work-design research finds strong associations between well-being/satisfaction and things like:

- **Autonomy**
- **Competence/mastery**
- **Social support**
- **Good manager / good colleagues**
- **Clear goals**
- **Task significance / meaning**
- **Reasonable workload**
- **Low organizational dysfunction**
- **Work-life balance**

The **Job Characteristics Model** literature and meta-analyses such as **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007** find that autonomy, feedback, social support, task significance, and related factors are strongly related to job satisfaction and motivation.

So “interesting research topic” is one dimension. But leading a small team may give you more autonomy, agency, mentoring, status, and skill growth — all of which can be satisfying even if the object-level research is less exciting.

### 3. People are bad at affective forecasting

There is a large literature on **affective forecasting** — e.g. work by **Daniel Gilbert, Timothy Wilson**, and others — showing that people often mispredict how much future circumstances will affect their happiness.

A common pattern is the **focusing illusion**: when you think hard about one feature of a future life, you overestimate how important it will be. Kahneman summarized this as:

> “Nothing in life is as important as you think it is while you are thinking about it.”

So when deciding, you may over-focus on “the work at Anthropic is more interesting” or “the OpenAI offer has higher salary/leadership” and underweight mundane daily factors: meetings, manager quality, colleagues, commute, decision rights, interruptions, how often you feel effective, etc.

### 4. Salary increases matter, but less at high income levels

Income is related to life satisfaction and, to some extent, experienced well-being. But at high incomes, marginal gains are usually smaller than people expect.

The older **Kahneman & Deaton 2010** result suggested emotional well-being plateaued around a certain income level, though later work by **Killingsworth 2021** and **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers 2023** found a more continuous relationship for many people. Still, the effect of extra income is generally much smaller than the effect of bad health, bad relationships, toxic work, chronic stress, or lack of autonomy.

So a somewhat higher salary should count, especially if it affects financial freedom, family plans, or risk tolerance. But it probably should not dominate if both packages are already very strong.

### 5. “Interesting” can mean two different things

This is important. There is:

1. **Object-level interestingness**: “The technical problem is fascinating.”
2. **Role-level interestingness**: “The role helps me grow, gives me agency, lets me shape people/projects, and teaches me new skills.”

You might be comparing:

- Anthropic: higher object-level research interest.
- OpenAI: potentially higher role-level growth/leadership interest.

If you have “always wanted to lead a small team,” that is not a trivial preference. Management/leadership can become its own deep craft: hiring, taste formation, prioritization, research direction, helping people grow, building culture, allocating attention. Some people find this more satisfying than IC work; others discover they miss doing the work directly.

### Practical implication for your decision

I would not frame the choice as:

> “Should I choose interesting work or money?”

I would frame it as:

> “Which role gives me the better expected day-to-day life, growth path, agency, and long-term regret profile?”

A useful exercise:

| Factor | Anthropic | OpenAI |
|---|---:|---:|
| Object-level research interest | ? | ? |
| Autonomy / decision rights | ? | ? |
| Manager quality | ? | ? |
| Team quality | ? | ? |
| Leadership growth | ? | ? |
| Mission/values fit | ? | ? |
| Work-life balance | ? | ? |
| Compensation / financial runway | ? | ? |
| Career capital / option value | ? | ? |
| Daily energy after 6 months | ? | ? |
| Regret if you decline | ? | ? |

Then ask yourself two sharper questions:

1. **If OpenAI paid the same as Anthropic, would I still be tempted because of the leadership role?**  
   If yes, the offer is not just about money.

2. **If the Anthropic work were only moderately more interesting, would I still stay?**  
   If yes, maybe your real reasons include mission, colleagues, trust, identity, or current momentum.

### Bottom line

There are indeed results suggesting that choosing the “most interesting possible job” is not a guaranteed route to much higher happiness. Interest fit matters, but usually modestly, and people often overpredict how much one salient job feature will affect well-being.

For your case, the leadership opportunity may be a genuinely important source of satisfaction and career growth. But you should investigate whether the OpenAI role is merely “less interesting” or actually misaligned with what gives you energy. A less fascinating project plus more agency could be great; a boring project plus lots of coordination overhead could be draining.

I’d put a lot of weight on the concrete daily reality: who your manager is, how much autonomy you have, what the team’s actual mandate is, how much time you spend doing research vs coordination, and whether the leadership path is real rather than nominal.
